diary:
Woke up to that soft, steady drizzle in Redmond, like the sky forgot to stop. Let the dog out around 6:20 and he immediately went full skeptic about the wet grass, then I made coffee and checked the commute timing like I always do when Seattle’s acting up. The SR-520 bridge closure had everyone talking, so I took the extra route toward Interbay and rolled in with that damp, half-annoyed feeling.

At the shipyard, the morning stayed gray and the air felt colder than 58F, but my hands worked fine—fabrication, inspections, checklists, and the quiet focus that welding demands. Lunch at The Pink Door warmed me up, and afterward I swung by QFC in Ballard for shop supplies and another coffee before planning tomorrow’s welds.

By the time I was out near Woodinville finishing up, my shoulders finally loosened. Board game night by Juanita Landing kept the laughs going even while the rain kept tapping the windows. I’m tired, but it feels like a good kind of tired.
thought_process: I adjusted my route early because the SR-520 eastbound closure was the one variable I could control; losing time in traffic would’ve thrown off my work pacing and made the rest of the day feel rushed. At the shipyard, I leaned into the structured parts—inspection checklists and fabrication steps—because damp conditions make everything feel slower and more finicky, so staying methodical helped me avoid mistakes. Lunch at The Pink Door wasn’t just food; it was a reset from the wet commute, a small warmth that kept my mood from sinking. The errand to QFC fit naturally into the buffer before afternoon weld planning, so I wouldn’t be scrambling later. The drizzle kept me low-key all day, but board games gave me a lift and ended the day in a calmer headspace.
world_event_prompt: Grey, drizzly Tuesday with steady rain from 7am to 2pm, high of 58F. SR-520 bridge closed eastbound 9am-noon for emergency repair, snarling cross-lake traffic.
